text.content:
Overview

The Little Palace is a small civic guesthouse maintained by the Audit Hall for sensitive visitors, diplomatic witnesses, and protected debriefings. Built in the early decades of the Republic from the same pale limestone as the city walls, it has been continuously occupied for four hundred years and renovated only enough to keep it functional. Whitewashed plaster inside, slate floors, dark beams. No heraldic excess. The "ceremonial leftovers" are quiet ones: a banner, a plaque, a glass case with a tarnished gavel.

Footprint: three floors. The main floor is the largest, with a row of small page rooms along the east wing. The second floor sits over the central core only — the wings beneath are roofed, not floored. The cellar predates the rest of the building; mortar in its lower courses is older than the building above.

Permanent staff

  • Elsbet Vael, household steward — late forties, former audit-hall clerk. Honest, capable, politically uninvolved. Holds one of the two Strong Room keys.
  • Brennan Kepf, cook — sixties, nineteen years' service. Treats the building as his own house.
  • Mira Holt, kitchen-girl — seventeen, three days in service, hired through the standard civic register on a recommendation from the local seamstress' guild. (See first-night complications.)
  • Two militia guards on rotating four-hour watches at the front portico.

Main Floor

M1 — North Portico & Entry Vestibule

A columned, torchlit approach steps up from the Civic Way to a pair of iron-bound oak doors. Inside the inner door, a small slate-floored vestibule holds a leather-bound visitor's register and a single inkpot. A militia guard stands at the inner door.

  • Investigation DC 13 on the register: the previous entry, two months old, is for a "trade attaché from the Free City of Dyvers" — recorded but unsigned. Unusual; signatures are required.
  • Ceremonial leftover: a faded gold-thread banner of the Yeomanry crest hangs above the inner door, embroidered with the older phrasing "By Our Hand and Our Common Counsel."

M2 — Reception Parlor

Off the entry vestibule, a smaller red-carpeted parlor for first greetings. Two settees, a sideboard with watered wine, a low fireplace. Walls hold three small unlabeled portraits of past Spokesmen.

  • Ceremonial leftover: a locked glass case displays a tarnished silver gavel from the Republic's first Council session.

M3 — Grand Salon

The central public reception of the main floor. A larger arrangement of sofas and chairs around a deeper hearth. Used for receptions of more than four guests, civic award ceremonies, and the recording of formal testimony when scribes are required. Public Congratulations in Scene 3 may be staged here if weather forces the welcome indoors.

M4 — Refectory

The dining room. Long oak table, ten chairs. White plaster walls, dark beams. A serving hatch wall connects to the kitchen. Above the hearth, a carved wooden plaque lists every person formally housed in the Little Palace since 4470 CY. The party's names will be added before they leave.

M5 — Library / Records Room

Open to guests. Yeomanry constitutional history, session minutes, regional newspapers (Folio of the Mere, Hardby Mercantile Weekly). Writing desk at the window. Bookshelves on every wall.

  • Investigation DC 13 in the Mercantile Weekly back-issues: a three-month-old notice that "underwriting requests from Rel Astran houses for Yeomanry-flagged charter hulls have been declined for the third quarter running."

M6 — Kitchen

Coal range, three water buckets, copper pots, prep table. Side door opens onto the alley. A small alcove off the kitchen serves as Mira's sleeping closet. The serving hatch to the Refectory (M4) is on the east wall.

  • Investigation DC 12 in the alley midden: a scrap of burnt vellum (see complication 5).

M7 — Scullery / Workroom

Adjacent to the kitchen. Washing basin, laundry copper, pegs for service aprons. Junior household help — bakers' aides, scullions — work here by day and leave at sundown.

M8 — Inner Courtyard & Garden

Cobbled path around a circular planter holding a quince tree, herb beds, and a stone bench. A sealed well sits under a wooden cover near the central planter. High walls on three sides; the building forms the fourth. A side gate at the south wall opens onto a service alley, locked at sundown. A militia guard patrols the alley on a slow loop, every twenty minutes.

M9 — Bath Room

A small private chamber on the east side, accessible from the page-room corridor. Stone-lined tub kept warm by a small under-floor flue from the kitchen hearth. Used by guests in turn, rationed by the steward.

M10 — Page Rooms (east wing)

Four identical small bedchambers in a row along the east wall, each with a single bed, a chest, a chair, and a tiny window. Intended for accompanying aides, page-boys, secretaries, or junior delegations traveling with senior visitors. The party is not housed here — they are in the senior suites upstairs (U3 and U5) — but a PC may share or trade with companions if they prefer to remain on the main floor for tactical reasons.

M11 — Main Stair Hall

A wide oak stair on the west side of the building rises to the second floor. Daylight from the upper hall floods down. Polished by four centuries of footfall. Lined with iron sconces.

M12 — Spiral Stair (Turret) & Service Yard

A narrow stone turret at the south-west corner houses a tight spiral stair descending to the cellar. Outside the turret, a small enclosed service yard contains a stone bench and a barrel; the wall holds a small carved memorial to the first Senior Auditor, scratched with the names of clerks who died in service. A small altar-like ledge at the east wall of the yard is used once a year on the Republic's founding day to mount a candle.

Second Floor

U1 — Upper Stair Landing & Hall

Top of the main stair. A long, dim hall paneled in dark walnut runs the length of the central core. Iron sconces every ten feet. Three doors on the south side, two on the north. A narrow archway at the south-east opens onto the open gallery (U6).

U2 — Caelith's Civic Office

A small, wood-paneled study near the head of the stair. Heavy desk facing the door; behind it a window onto the inner courtyard. Two visitors' chairs. A small bookcase. A locked correspondence cupboard (key on Caelith's belt). A plain table to one side serves for short, informal lunches when civic guests stay the day.

  • Ceremonial leftover: a framed worn ribbon-and-medallion of the original "Keeper of the Common Word," eighth-century precursor to Caelith's modern post, hangs above the desk.

This is where Caelith reads the Private Warning (Scene 5).

U3 — Senior Guest Suite "Mere"

The largest upper room. Carved four-poster bed, a writing desk with a quill stand, a washstand, an upholstered chair before a small private hearth. Window onto the inner courtyard. Door has an interior bolt only.

U4 — Upper Salon (Old Council Chamber leftover)

Centrally placed. Six chairs ring a low central hearth instead of facing it; the layout preserves the seating geometry of an early Republic council circle, even though the salon is now used as a private upper sitting room. The hearth carries the soot of four hundred winters. A small glass case in one corner holds a folded silk banner of the first Spokesman's livery. Useful as a private conversation space if Caelith's office is too formal; the steward will unlock it on request.

U5 — Senior Guest Suite "Stark"

Mirror of U3, smaller. Single bed, writing desk, washstand. Window on the front street. Door has interior bolt only.

U6 — Open Gallery

A short walled gallery on the south side of the second floor opens to the inner courtyard below. Stone railing. Used for taking morning air without leaving the building. The magpie complication (#4) plays out here or at U3's window.

Cellar

The cellar predates the present footprint by an unknown number of decades. The vaulted ceilings are stone; lighting is by lantern only.

C1 — Spiral Stair Foot

The base of the turret stair from M12. A narrow stone landing with iron-bound doors to the wine cellar (C2) and the barrel hall (C4).

C2 — Wine Cellar

A long, narrow room lined floor-to-ceiling with wine racks. The collection is modest but old; many bottles bear the wax-seal of vineyards no longer in operation. An inventory ledger on a small writing slope at the door is kept up to date by the cook.

C3 — Provisions Cellar

Crates, salt-meat barrels, sacks of flour and pulses, hanging rounds of cheese. A small ice-box sunk into the cool stone floor. Doors to the barrel hall (C4) and the cistern chamber (C6).

C4 — Barrel Hall

The largest cellar room. Three rows of barrels — beer, cider, wine seconds — and a long central aisle. Stacked crates and wooden boxes line the walls. The cook's notes mark which barrels are tapped and which are sealed.

C5 — Strong Room

A small stone-walled chamber with a reinforced iron-bound door and two locks (Caelith and Elsbet each hold one; both must turn). No window. Two shelves of sealed crates and one open chest containing Haskur's gear, set out for the party's examination during Scene 5. Caelith does not allow items to leave the room without his witness.

Scene 5's evidence examination occurs here. Caelith leads the party from his Civic Office (U2) down through the spiral stair (M12 → C1) to this room, carrying a candle.

C6 — Cistern Chamber

A vaulted room with a stone basin at the center; rainwater is collected from the rooftop via a clay drain and stored here for the building's drinking and washing supply. The water is cold and clean. A ladle hangs on a chain. An old brass plaque on the wall, dated 4082 CY, reads "The Republic Endures By Its Smallest Disciplines."

C7 — Old Service Tunnel (GM-only)

The east wall of the cellar's lower passage opens — through a stone arch reinforced with timber — into a curving tunnel that leaves the building footprint and continues east underground. The walls are older than the rest of the cellar. The floor is dirt over flagstone; recent footprints are visible if a PC bothers to look.

  • Investigation DC 13 at the tunnel mouth: two sets of recent footprints, one going in, one going out, both within the last three days. The boot tread is plain and unremarkable.
  • History DC 15: a civic-infrastructure tunnel from the Republic's earliest decades; many early stone buildings of Loftwick connected to a common drainage network that has since been mostly walled off above ground. The Audit Hall, two streets east, almost certainly has a corresponding (once-sealed) entrance.

GM: Do not resolve where the tunnel goes in Phase 1. The clear implication — that someone has been entering or leaving the Little Palace through the cellar without using the main door, and that this someone may also have access to the Audit Hall — is enough. This is the structural mechanism by which Caelith's office could be leaking. The party can investigate further in Phase 2 or beyond. If they ask Caelith about it, he says only: "I have walked it once. I did not enjoy what I saw at the other end. I would prefer that you not walk it tonight."

pages.name: Map Index — Main Floor, Second Floor, Cellar

text.content:
Three rendered maps live in assets/maps/. Each is gridless; Foundry overlays its scene grid at runtime. Three matching scenes are shipped in the Phase 1 — Scenes compendium.

FileFloorImage sizeFoundry scene
little-palace-main-floor.pngMain floor (M1–M12)1448 × 1086Little Palace — Main Floor
little-palace-2nd-floor.pngSecond floor (U1–U6)1448 × 1086Little Palace — Second Floor
little-palace-cellar.pngCellar (C1–C7)1448 × 1086Little Palace — Cellar

Aligning the grid in Foundry

The shipped scenes set grid: { type: square, distance: 5, units: ft, size: 100 } as a starting point. The artist did not necessarily render at exactly 100 px per 5-ft square; use Foundry's Align Grid Tool to dial the size to whatever the painted floorplan actually uses.

  1. Open the scene's configuration sheet (Scene Navigation → right-click → Configure).
  2. Under Grid Configuration, confirm Grid Type is Square and Grid Distance is 5 feet.
  3. Click the Align Grid Tool button. Click two points on the map known to be a fixed number of feet apart — e.g. the inner faces of two opposing walls in the courtyard (M8 is roughly 25 × 20 ft) or the long oak table in the Refectory (M4 is roughly 6 × 2 ft, eight chairs around it). Foundry computes the right pixel-per-square value automatically.
  4. Save. The grid will land on whole-square boundaries and tokens will move correctly.

Walls, lighting, and tokens

The shipped scenes have no walls and no placed lights. For production play:

  • Walls: hand-draw walls along stone partitions in Foundry's Walls layer. Mark doors as door-type walls so they swing. Foundry's Generate Walls assist (Scene Configuration → Walls) can give you a starting trace from high-contrast art.
  • Daytime ambient: globalLight off; broad-radius bright tiles at each window.
  • Night ambient: hearth glow tile in M3 / U4 (radius 4), candle in U2 (radius 2), lantern at the spiral-stair turret M12 / C1 (radius 3), wall-sconces along U1 hall (radius 2 each).

Tactical features worth marking on each map

Main floor

  • Service alley door at M6 kitchen (locked at sundown).
  • South gate at M8 courtyard (locked at sundown; militia patrol passes every 20 minutes).
  • Main stair (M11) ascends to U1; spiral stair (M12) descends to C1.

Second floor

  • U2 (Caelith's office) and U6 (open gallery) face the courtyard. The magpie complication (#4) plays at one of these windows.
  • U4 is normally locked; the steward Elsbet Vael opens it on request.

Cellar

  • C5 (Strong Room) is double-locked; Caelith and Elsbet each hold one key.
  • C7 (Old Service Tunnel) is the GM-only feature — the structural mechanism by which an antagonist could enter or leave the building unobserved. Do not resolve in Phase 1.
